stories.title: European AI stocks soar with global tech frenzy

body.text: A small group of European AI stocks has posted huge gains this year as investors hunt for ways to ride a global investment boom in a continent often derided for its lack of large technology companies. The top four performers in the region-wide Stoxx Europe 600 index this year are chipmaker STMicroelectronics, which has more than doubled in value; semiconductor equipment manufacturers Aixtron, up 168 per cent, and BE Semiconductor Industries, up 87 per cent; and Nokia, up nearly 100 per cent.

The Stoxx Europe Total Market Semiconductors index is up about 74 per cent this year, while the broad Stoxx Europe 600 has gained barely 2 per cent. "Whoever is investing in Europe is desperate to get a claim on this AI trade," said Emmanuel Cau of Barclays, pointing to a "gold rush".

Kasper Elmgreen of Nordea Asset Management added that investors' focus in Europe has shifted away from the so-called "Granolas" — 11 large healthcare and luxury stocks — towards the continent's drive for strategic autonomy, including in technology.

stories.title: Global oil inventories plunge at record pace, risking price spikes

body.text: In its monthly report, the International Energy Agency (IEA) warned that global oil inventories are being depleted at a record pace due to continued disruption to Middle Eastern supplies from the Iran war. Stockpiles of crude oil and refined fuel fell by almost 4mn barrels a day in April — more than the UK and Germany's consumption combined. Since the start of the war, global oil stocks have plummeted by almost 250mn barrels. "Rapidly shrinking buffers amid continued disruptions may herald future price spikes ahead," the IEA said.

The Strait of Hormuz normally carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil; the reduced flow has caused the largest supply disruption in history. Europe will cut consumption by 140,000 barrels a day this year. The IEA's forecast assumes the war ends in early June. Investment bank Goldman Sachs speaks of an "unprecedented supply shock"; visible inventories shrank by 8.7mn barrels a day in May.

Commerzbank economists estimate the market is short roughly 13mn barrels a day, a good 12 per cent of global demand; combined reserves would last about 15 months, though some oil products could become tight as early as summer. Exports through the strait remain at a very low five per cent of normal.
The UN warns that the number of people facing acute hunger could reach a record level this year because of the war, as disruption to natural gas supplies pushes up fertiliser prices.

stories.title: SpaceX IPO set to lock in Musk's control with Mars-linked pay

body.text: SpaceX has proposed one of the most aggressive corporate governance structures in US history, intended to guarantee that Elon Musk cannot be fired, and is offering him a trillion-dollar pay deal linked to building a colony on Mars. The structure has not deterred investors, who are overlooking weak shareholder rights for a chance to own a "unique" company headed for the largest ever IPO. Musk is targeting a listing next month to coincide with a rare alignment of planets, his 55th birthday on June 28 and ahead of the US's 250th anniversary on July 4. The rocketmaker has discussed raising about $75bn at a $1.75tn valuation.

Musk would control a supermajority of class B shares carrying tenfold voting power; he already owns at least 40 per cent of the shares and more than 80 per cent of voting rights. He will serve as both chief executive and chairman of the nine-member board. The pension funds of New York and California voiced "serious concerns" about what they called the most management-favouring governance structure ever brought to the US public market on such a scale.

stories.title: McKinsey to cut cash share of partner pay in response to AI

body.text: McKinsey plans to cut the share of profits that partners take home in cash, in an overhaul designed to simplify its pay structure, boost its capital and protect the firm from turmoil in the consulting industry. Partners have been told to expect a greater proportion of their pay in the form of equity. For some partners, the changes could affect tens of thousands of dollars. The revamp — codenamed Project Acorn — will benefit younger partners by bringing forward some pay that would previously not have been allocated for several years.

The firm is rethinking its pay structure because clients are increasingly tying fees to the savings or performance improvements consultants achieve, and the trend is accelerating as AI revolutionises the industry. Consulting groups are embracing outcome-based pricing to counter client scepticism. McKinsey partner pay typically consists of a base salary, a performance bonus and a share of profits called the "additional award". The share of that bonus diverted into equity could rise by an estimated 3 to 5 percentage points.

stories.title: Gulf conflict pushes European chemicals to the brink, Turkey raises inflation target

body.text: In the Port of Rotterdam, one of the world's largest chemical clusters, two of its 10 companies have shut down plants in the past year as the European chemicals industry is buffeted by high energy prices, weak demand and intensifying competition from China. The Middle East conflict has offered something of a reprieve by disrupting Chinese plants that rely on feedstock from the Gulf, but it has also pushed energy costs higher and stoked price volatility for critical inputs such as naphtha. "Developments in the Middle East are pushing energy costs even higher," said Peter Huntsman, chief executive of Huntsman Corporation. The number of plant shutdowns in Europe has risen sixfold in four years.

Turkey's central bank has sharply increased its annual inflation target as the country's stabilisation programme comes under growing strain, exacerbated by the Iran war. It lifted its 2026 inflation target to 24 per cent from 16 per cent, and almost doubled next year's target to 15 per cent. Turkey imports almost three-quarters of its energy needs. Households expect prices to climb 52 per cent and businesses anticipate a 33 per cent increase. In March the country suffered a record $43bn decline in foreign reserves. The main interest rate remains at 37 per cent, and Governor Fatih Karahan said the bank did not plan to change its monetary policy.

stories.title: Klarna turns its first profit since IPO

body.text: Swedish fintech company Klarna has turned a profit for the first time since its $15bn New York listing last September.

In the first quarter it reported $1mn in net income, compared with a net loss of $99mn in the same period last year. Revenue reached $1bn, up 44 per cent, helped by a surge in sign-ups for its debit card. Klarna became known for its buy now, pay later services, but is now shifting towards a more traditional digital bank model, and according to earlier FT reports is considering applying for an American banking licence. The company set aside $186mn for credit losses, nearly 40 per cent more than last year. Payment volume (GMV) rose by a third to $33.7bn, while active users climbed 21 per cent to 119mn. Since its IPO the shares have lost 65 per cent, leaving a valuation of $5.9bn, but after the results were published the stock rose 15 per cent.

stories.title: Cuts to US food benefits hit grocery sales

body.text: The food industry is starting to feel the impact of cuts to US grocery subsidies that were part of last year's Republican tax and spending bill. Kraft Heinz and retailer Ahold Delhaize said lower demand from recipients of the Snap program had dented sales.

Kraft Heinz, the producer of Kraft macaroni and cheese and Oscar Mayer meats, estimates a 1 percentage point hit to its rate of revenue growth this year. Households enrolled in Snap make almost a third more shopping trips than other households and spend largely on branded products. Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act will cut federal Snap spending by nearly $187bn over ten years. The program paid out $95bn last year, averaging $188 per month per recipient. After work requirements were tightened, including for people aged 55 to 64, the number of recipients has fallen by 3.5mn, or 8 per cent, since last July.

stories.title: OpenAI reworks its gigantic $500bn Stargate project

body.text: OpenAI's $500bn Stargate plan to secure computing power is being reworked and, in places, abandoned.

In the past months, the company has halted planned data centres in the UK and Norway, declined to expand its flagship site in Abilene, Texas, and seen several senior figures tied to Stargate leave for rival Meta. The project was originally announced in early 2025 by President Trump as a joint venture between OpenAI, Oracle, Abu Dhabi fund MGX and Japan's SoftBank. The joint-venture concept has given way to a more flexible approach, with OpenAI increasingly leasing capacity from third parties rather than building and owning its own facilities. Oracle plays the most prominent role, having committed last year to deliver 4.5 gigawatts of computing capacity under a five-year, $300bn deal. The company has also partnered with AMD, Broadcom, Nvidia, CoreWeave and Cerebras, at one point exceeding a trillion dollars in commitments.

stories.title: Honda posts first-ever annual loss after pullback from E.V.s

body.text: Honda Motor on Thursday reported its first annual loss since becoming a publicly traded company in Japan seven decades ago. For the fiscal year that ended March 31 it posted a net loss of $2.7 billion.

Earnings were weighed down by more than $9 billion in restructuring charges and write-downs after a retrenchment of its E.V. strategy. It is the first loss that the 77-year-old company has reported since listing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange in 1957. "The business environment and customer demand have changed beyond our expectations," said chief executive Toshihiro Mibe. Just five years ago, Honda was racing to catch up to Tesla and Chinese rivals such as BYD, and pledged to make its entire lineup electric or hydrogen-powered by 2040. Since 2021 it has invested billions in battery vehicles, including in collaboration with General Motors and Sony. But demand for electric vehicles has cooled.

stories.title: New York plans tax surcharge on the rich's second homes

body.text: Gov. Kathy Hochul on Thursday proposed to state legislative leaders a new tax surcharge on the second homes of wealthy part-time residents of New York City. It would be instituted in two phases.

For the next two years, second homes with a "market value" of at least $1 million will face a tax surcharge between 4 percent and 6.5 percent, in addition to their existing property tax obligations. This "market value" is a bureaucratic metric that often vastly understates a home's actual worth. According to the governor's office, a $1 million "market value" is equivalent to a sales value of about $5 million, but the disparity is often even more pronounced. One Manhattan penthouse with a market value of about $4.2 million sold in 2024 for more than $135 million. The tax is expected to generate $500 million a year for the city. Real estate broker Jason Haber called the structure needlessly complicated.

stories.title: Czech investors buy another shopping center in Poland, in Lodz

body.text: The domestic private equity firm Crestpoint Capital Partners has bought the Pasaz Lódzki shopping center for a private Czech investor in Poland's third-largest city, Lodz.

It is the largest commercial real estate acquisition to date by the firm, founded two years ago by Tomáš Jandík, the former head of the Reico real estate fund from the Erste Group. Among the center's key tenants are the French chains Auchan and Decathlon, along with roughly 60 other brands including TK Maxx, HalfPrice and CCC. Opened in 2000, the center covers 36,400 square meters and is visited by 3.5 million people a year. Crestpoint did not disclose the price; according to HN's information, the value is around 50 million euros (1.2 billion crowns). Last month the Czech group SCF announced the purchase of a shopping center in Slupsk. In the first quarter, commercial real estate worth 1.05 billion euros was traded in Poland.

stories.title: The state returns to borrowing from households, presents Republic Bonds

body.text: The government plans a budget deficit of 310 billion crowns for this year and wants to borrow from Czech households as well to cover the gap. It is thus returning to the practice of 2018 to 2022, when it raised nearly 80 billion from people. The Ministry of Finance presented the parameters of the Republic Bond with maturities ranging from three months to five years. "There is enormous interest in Czech government bonds," said Finance Minister Alena Schillerová (ANO).

People can invest in a single issue from 1,000 crowns up to three million, theoretically up to nine million during the first subscription period. Schillerová expects sales of roughly 20 billion crowns. The first option is a five-year fixed bond with a yield to maturity of 4.5 percent. The second is an anti-inflation bond, whose yield is set annually according to the consumer price index; the yield on early redemption will be capped at five percent, and the premium for holding to maturity is 2.5 percent.

stories.title: A wave of layoffs is hitting Germany, the auto industry is the worst off

body.text: The labor market in Germany is undergoing dramatic changes. Private companies, as part of tough austerity programs, plan to cut thousands of jobs and many would like to move part of their production to countries with a more favorable cost structure. According to an analysis by Manager Magazin, job losses threaten across the entire economy, with the greatest attention focused on the auto industry, whose position in the world has weakened due to pressure from Asian competition. According to Handelsblatt, the margins of BMW, Mercedes and Volkswagen are roughly at pandemic-era levels.

The trio reported a combined operating EBIT profit of 6.4 billion euros in the first quarter, 23 percent less year-on-year. Volkswagen, Europe's largest automaker, faces the toughest austerity program in its history. Around 1,400 jobs in the group's research arm, the subsidiary IAV, are also at risk. The maker Porsche, where the crisis is deepest, has already announced staff cuts.

stories.title: How dangerous is the flood of private credit?

body.text: After the turbulence at the end of March, the situation in private credit has calmed down, but the discussions continue. At their core is the question of the extent to which private credit poses a danger to the international credit economy, and whether it could be the seed of a financial crisis. Its significance has grown immensely since the financial crisis of the 2000s. Robert Fauber, head of the rating agency Moody's, puts the volume of capital in private-credit funds at 16 trillion dollars, to which is added a financing ecosystem of more than one trillion dollars.

At the end of 2024, public pension and sovereign funds put nearly every fifth dollar into non-listed equities, credit or real estate, with some of the largest funds aiming for quotas of 40 percent. Moody's puts the exposure of US banks at the end of 2025 at 1.4 trillion dollars. Competition between direct lenders and banks lowered yield spreads by about a percentage point last year, which brings looser credit standards. The lack of transparency remains the main topic.

stories.title: Africa is not a blanket high-risk region, experts say

body.text: The perception of the African continent as a blanket high-risk space lags behind economic reality, was the message at a round table with the president of the African Development Bank. Africa is supposed to become strategically more important, yet financially it is still too often treated, across the board, as high-risk. African states and companies pay on international capital markets not only for real risks but often also for overly crude rating formulas that fail to account for differences between countries, regions and sectors.

This is shown by the OECD's country risk classification: 44 of the 51 recorded African states lie in the highest risk bands, which suggests a homogeneity that does not correspond to the continent's economic and institutional reality. The OECD itself, however, defines the scope of this instrument narrowly — it serves only as a building block for minimum premiums on state-backed export credits. The authors point out that African countries offer sales markets and investment opportunities.

stories.title: Government considers cutting electricity prices, but does not want to lower the excise duty on diesel

body.text: Amid rising fuel prices, calls for cutting the excise duty are growing, but according to Deputy Prime Minister Karel Havlíček the cabinet is not preparing any such step.

The price of a litre of diesel at filling stations in the Czech Republic exceeded 44 crowns on average, reaching levels last seen in 2022. Should the situation become unsustainable for consumers, the government is ready to help through relief on the price of electricity. If the state wants to lower end prices, it will have to reach into the regulated component — the costs of system services, distribution or grid losses. This would not be a novelty: in 2023 Fiala's government approved a subsidy of 22.7 billion crowns for transmission system operator ČEPS and another 15 billion for the trio of distribution companies. The President of the European Commission Ursula von der Leyen also called on member states to lower energy fees.

stories.title: Indebted Solek comes up with a new reorganisation plan

body.text: Creditors of the indebted solar group Solek Holding can now examine a new reorganisation plan that the company has drawn up in order to avoid bankruptcy. They still hope that Solek has a chance to keep operating and at least slightly satisfy some of the creditors.

A court-ordered expert appraisal valued the group's assets at a mere 83 million crowns, that is 1.5 percent of the sum Solek itself stated in the insolvency petition. The agreed investment group ENPO Group subsequently revised its offer of help from 250 million to just 30 million crowns, and no longer speaks of further possible aid worth half a billion. The company built large solar parks abroad, with a key part of its business in Chile, where it ran into economic trouble as a result of a regulatory change. This gradually dragged down the entire holding, which was highly indebted and could not pay out its bonds.

body.text: One of the most significant deals in the field of workwear and protective equipment has been officially approved and completed. Cerva Group is among the major players in the field of personal protective equipment and work gear. The combination of the two companies will create a stronger entity on the European market for workwear and protective equipment.

stories.title: After 15 years, municipalities are again investing more instead of stockpiling surpluses

body.text: Finance ministers regularly point out that municipalities accumulate budget surpluses in their accounts. Last year, however, was exceptional in this respect — municipalities invested more than usual and, after excluding Prague, ended up in the red for the first time in fifteen years.

Municipal revenues last year reached 386 billion crowns, but overall they spent 9.3 billion more, and their investment expenditure jumped by 26 percent. According to the Ministry of Finance, municipalities invested a quarter more capital than the year before, mainly in education, sport, housing and transport. The ministry partly explains the higher intensity of spending by the pre-election year — municipal elections take place this October. One example is the municipality of Nové Hutě with 90 inhabitants, which after thirty years began building a wastewater treatment plant for more than 70 million crowns. Prague last year ended with its second-largest surplus in history, of 24 billion crowns.

stories.title: After the budget signing, an unprecedented wave of signing transport contracts gets underway

body.text: Only a few hours have passed since the president signed the law on the state budget, and feverish activity has broken out among the state infrastructure administrators. The uncertainty over budgets had lasted since the first draft in September, that is roughly half a year.

The head of the Road and Motorway Directorate (ŘSD) Radek Mátl already issued the first instructions to start construction during Monday — the major extension of the D11 motorway by almost twenty kilometres to Trutnov will, according to him, begin on 1 April, with the contract worth roughly 9.1 billion won by a consortium led by Eurovia CZ. Among the priorities are also the reconstruction of the Aviatická junction at the start of the D7 near Prague airport and the D35 Úlibice–Hořice section. At the Railway Administration, the final budget was cut considerably more, from 87 to a final 72 billion. The main thing awaited is the signing of the contract for a new line from Kladno to the edge of Prague worth roughly eight billion crowns.

stories.title: AI boom drives demand for "flying developers"

body.text: Amid the AI boom, companies are massively seeking so-called forward deployed engineers, that is "flying developers" who "move in" directly to customers for a few weeks or months. According to American media, this is currently the most frequently opened job position in Silicon Valley.

These field IT consultants help end-user companies integrate AI tools into complex corporate systems and also advise on data security and legal compliance. Andrej Hájek, head of the Czech development company FLO, says the product does not sell like boxed software.

The Slovak-Czech company CloudTalk is also interested in the role, with its head Martin Malych describing it as a strategic step. Barry McCardel, who held the role for five years at Palantir, told the Wall Street Journal that "it's not pretty and it's not for everyone." People face pressure from clients with high expectations, compensated by high rewards.

shortBody.text: Amid the AI boom, companies are massively seeking so-called forward deployed engineers, that is "flying developers" who "move in" directly to customers for a few weeks or months. According to American media, this is currently the most frequently opened job position in Silicon Valley.

stories.title: InPost CEO wants to erase logistics borders in Europe

body.text: InPost, one of the most successful companies from the former Eastern bloc, plans to connect logistics networks across Europe and globally to the standard of the local market. In an interview with HN, its head describes a vision in which a customer would download an app similar to Uber, get a QR code, scan it at a locker and insert a parcel. Delivery would cost around three or four pounds.

Today, according to him, sending a parcel from London to Prague via UPS or DHL means printing a label, paying about 25 euros and waiting eight to ten days. InPost wants to expand using franchises, "something like McDonald's," and to respect local regulations — in Italy, every location requires an architectural plan.
In Poland, and soon in the United Kingdom, it places publicly usable defibrillators in lockers and pays attention to accessibility.

stories.title: Trump floats Taiwan arms-sales talks in Beijing

body.text: Donald Trump has sparked alarm in Taipei and among Asian allies by saying, ahead of his first visit to China since 2017, that he would discuss American arms sales to Taiwan with Xi Jinping. "President Xi would like us not to, and I'll have that discussion," Trump said, breaking decades of precedent on not consulting Beijing on the matter.

Five months ago, Washington unveiled a record $11.1bn arms package for Taiwan and is preparing another worth at least $14bn. At the two-day summit in the Great Hall of the People, Xi told Trump that "the Taiwan question is the most important issue in China-US relations" and that, if mishandled, it could lead to "confrontation or even conflict." Xi also praised progress in trade talks and said there were "no winners" in a tariff war. Taiwan's foreign ministry described Beijing as "the sole risk to regional peace."

"President Xi would like us not to, and I'll have that discussion," Trump said, breaking decades of precedent on not consulting Beijing on the matter.

stories.title: Putin and Zelenskyy lose faith in US-led peace talks

body.text: Neither Russia nor Ukraine sees much prospect for reviving US-brokered peace talks, even after the war in the Middle East ends, according to people briefed on both sides' positions. Vladimir Putin has shifted focus to capturing more Ukrainian territory by force and intends to expand his demands further once Russia controls the key Donbas region. Last week Moscow said further talks were pointless until Ukraine withdraws from Donbas.

Kyiv, meanwhile, believes it is less vulnerable to US pressure for a quick, unfavourable deal after halting Russia's advance and inflicting damage deep behind enemy lines. "There has been zero progress secured by the American side from Russia," one Ukrainian official said. Trump had said last week that "we are getting closer and closer every day" to a deal after brokering a brief ceasefire.

stories.title: Lebanon and Israel meet in Washington over ceasefire extension

body.text: Lebanon and Israel are set to hold another round of direct talks in Washington focused on extending a ceasefire due to expire at the end of this week. It will be the third such meeting since March 2, when the two states were plunged back into war as Hizbollah began firing on northern Israel. Lebanese envoys are attending despite fierce opposition from the militant movement. Despite the truce, Israel and Hizbollah have continued to exchange fire on a daily basis.

Israeli attacks have killed more than 400 people in Lebanon over the past three weeks, including journalists, medical workers and soldiers, according to the country's ministry of health. On Wednesday alone, at least 22 people were killed, including eight children. Hizbollah said it carried out 17 attacks on Israeli forces using armed drones; one on Thursday wounded several Israeli civilians. Since the ceasefire, one Israeli civilian and five soldiers have been killed.

Lebanese envoys are attending despite fierce opposition from the militant movement. Despite the truce, Israel and Hizbollah have continued to exchange fire on a daily basis.

stories.title: Ukrainian arms maker aims to cut Kyiv's reliance on US

body.text: One of Ukraine's leading weapons manufacturers, Fire Point, says it launched two satellites this year and plans "dozens" more in 2027, as the country seeks to reduce its reliance on the US government and western tech companies. Co-founder and chief designer Denys Shtilierman said the company was expanding production of cruise and ballistic missiles and developing a "pan-European air-defence shield." "We sell not only weapons and not only security, but independence in security," he said.

Ukraine has stepped up domestic innovation after last year's Oval Office clash between Trump and Zelenskyy, which prompted the US to briefly suspend intelligence sharing. The army's reliance on the Starlink satellite system has also proved a vulnerability. Fire Point faces controversy, with its managers accused of using ties to Zelenskyy to win contracts and caught up in an anti-corruption investigation that has disrupted production and prompted calls to nationalise the company.

stories.title: Saudi Arabia floats non-aggression pact with Iran as monarchies strike Tehran

body.text: Saudi Arabia has discussed the idea of a non-aggression pact with Iran as part of talks with Middle East allies on how to manage regional tensions once the US-Israeli war with the Islamic republic ends, diplomats said. Riyadh was looking at the 1970s Helsinki Process, which eased tensions in Europe during the cold war, as a potential model; the Helsinki accords were signed in 1975 by the US, European countries and the Soviet Union. Many European capitals have backed the Saudi idea and are pressing other Gulf states. The US and Iran are holding secret talks to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, focused on the nuclear programme rather than the missile arsenal.
Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates separately carried out retaliatory strikes on Iran, the first known time the two countries have directly attacked the Islamic republic, US officials said.

Saudi Arabia has nearly 300,000 troops in service and, with seven percent of GDP, ranks among the countries with the highest defence spending. The Gulf states want Iran to emerge from the conflict militarily weakened to the point that it no longer poses a threat to the region.

stories.title: Merz rejects new joint EU debt despite Germany's €780bn rearmament drive

body.text: German Chancellor Friedrich Merz has vowed to oppose any future joint EU borrowing, even though his government has launched a €780bn debt-funded rearmament programme. According to Merz, the bloc needs to reprioritise its budget rather than take on fresh debt — and Germany cannot follow that path for constitutional reasons.

He warned that enough countries already spend more on interest than on defence due to immense debt. His remarks set up a confrontation with Paris over the EU's next seven-year budget from 2028; France, whose debt exceeds 115 per cent of GDP, is pushing for a larger budget and fresh joint borrowing for defence.
In 2020 the EU agreed to raise up to €800bn in joint debt to help economies recover from the Covid-19 shock, with Germany agreeing on the condition that it would be a one-off measure.

stories.title: US Supreme Court allows abortion pill access by mail to continue

body.text: The US Supreme Court ruled on Thursday that the widely used abortion medication mifepristone could continue to be prescribed by telehealth and sent to patients by mail. Two manufacturers of the drug had asked the court to intervene after the conservative Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals restricted access to the medication nationwide.

The brief order means the Fifth Circuit's decision will remain blocked while litigation continues. The majority did not explain its reasons; Justices Clarence Thomas and Samuel A. Alito Jr. dissented, with Alito calling the order "remarkable" and complaining it was "unreasoned."

The Fifth Circuit ruled on May 1, in response to a lawsuit filed by the State of Louisiana seeking to reimpose a requirement that patients obtain the medication only after seeing a provider in person. Nearly two-thirds of US abortions are now carried out with pills, about a quarter via telehealth, and more than 100 studies have found mifepristone to be safe.

stories.title: Pentagon abruptly cancels deployment of 4,000 troops to Poland

body.text: The US Defense Department has abruptly canceled the deployment of more than 4,000 troops to Poland, three US Army officials said, part of a realignment of American forces in Europe. Two weeks ago the Pentagon said it was withdrawing 5,000 troops from Germany and redeploying them to the United States and other posts; it also canceled a plan to place a missile-equipped artillery unit in Europe.

Those decisions came after President Trump angrily responded to remarks by Chancellor Friedrich Merz that Iran had "humiliated" the United States. There are now about 7,400 US troops in Poland, and the Army's V Corps has its forward headquarters in Poznan.

It was unclear why Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth canceled the deployment. Trump said the cuts would go deeper than 5,000 troops and threatened to pull forces from Italy and Spain too, saying they had not supported the administration's goals in the war with Iran.

stories.title: Starmer faces likely Labour leadership contest as health secretary resigns

body.text: British Prime Minister Keir Starmer now appears set to face at least two possible challengers for the Labour Party leadership. Health Secretary Wes Streeting resigned on Thursday but did not announce an immediate bid — he would need the backing of 81 Labour MPs.

Political momentum shifted to another rival: Andy Burnham, the popular mayor of Greater Manchester, announced he would run in a special election for a seat in Parliament, and if he wins he is expected to try to oust Starmer from No. 10 Downing Street. "Much bigger change is needed at a national level if everyday life is to be made more affordable again," Burnham said on X.

The moves by Streeting, who is to Starmer's right, and Burnham, to his left, were the most significant declarations of no confidence since the party's heavy losses in elections a week ago. Nearly 100 Labour MPs have called on Starmer to step down. A third possible rival, Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner, said she had been cleared after failing to pay the correct tax rate on a flat.

stories.title: Argentina races to find origin of cruise-ship hantavirus contagion

body.text: Argentine authorities are racing to trace the origin of a hantavirus contagion that struck a cruise ship and unnerved a world still scarred by the coronavirus pandemic. After unconfirmed theories spread that the contagion had originated in a landfill in the southern city of Ushuaia, where the boat departed, local authorities quickly pushed back.

"It's a maneuver to smear the image of Ushuaia as a tourist destination," said Martín Alfaro of the provincial health ministry, suggesting that operators in neighboring Chile might have spread rumors. The two first known victims — a Dutch couple — had embarked on a sprawling voyage across Argentina, Chile and Uruguay, making it hard for investigators to trace the origin of the infection.

The Andes species of the hantavirus, which can be transmitted between people, is endemic in three provinces of Argentine Patagonia. The Argentine ministry said the couple had not visited those areas during the incubation period. Chile ruled out infection on its territory, a claim Argentine spokesman Federico Lada rejected.

stories.title: Appeals court skeptical of Trump orders targeting law firms

body.text: A federal appeals court on Thursday appeared skeptical of the constitutionality of the Trump administration's efforts to punish major law firms.

Months after taking office, Trump signed a series of executive orders imposing penalties against major law firms that had previously employed or done legal work for the president's political opposition. The orders canceled their federal contracts, revoked lawyers' security clearances and barred the firms' staff from entering federal buildings. A few firms cut deals with the administration, agreeing to do free legal work toward the president's goals in exchange for relief. But several firms — including Perkins Coie, WilmerHale, Susman Godfrey and Jenner & Block — balked and sued. The hearing took place in front of three judges of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit. The Justice Department first told the appeals court that it would abandon its appeal, then reversed that decision. Paul D. Clement, representing the firms, told the judges that the orders violated "a good chunk of the Bill of Rights."

stories.title: Justice Dept. accuses Yale Medical School of discriminating against white and Asian applicants

body.text: The Justice Department on Thursday accused the Yale School of Medicine of violating anti-discrimination laws, the second major medical school targeted in the past eight days by the Trump administration over admissions policies the government said illegally favored Black and Hispanic applicants over more qualified white and Asian students.

Last week, the Justice Department issued similar findings for the David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLA. In March, the department opened investigations into admissions policies at Stanford, Ohio State and UC San Diego, and in February it sued Harvard. Harmeet K. Dhillon, the assistant attorney general for civil rights, said Yale was subverting a 2023 Supreme Court decision that overturned race-conscious admissions policies by relying on "proxies" to determine the ethnicity of its applicants. Yale said it was confident in its "rigorous admissions process."

stories.title: The first guest at the hacienda linked to Bödör was the owner's sister

body.text: Denník N found a tourist who managed to stay at the hacienda linked to Norbert Bödör. With her family she spent the night at the Vidiecky dom Malanta in Nitrianske Hrnčiarovce, which, thanks to EU funding, was supposed to become an accommodation facility for tourists, but in practice is inaccessible to the public and became known as an "impregnable fortress." Since last year, prospective guests have not been able to get inside, and the newspaper's fourth attempt also failed.

More successful five years ago was Diana Kevelyová with her husband, who live in Babindol just 14 kilometers from Malanta. For two nights in November 2021 they paid a thousand euros, and two weeks later they returned and spent another thousand euros. Kevelyová is the sister of the hacienda's official owner, Štefan Levický — both have permanent residence at the same family house in Babindol. They stayed at Malanta as the very first guests, as emerged from the guest book submitted to the PPA inspectors.

stories.title: At the Prešov hospital, bodies of the deceased were stored on shelves among boxes

body.text: The construction of a new military hospital in Prešov — currently halted, probably due to poor-quality concrete — is also affecting the operation of the J. A. Reiman Faculty Hospital with Polyclinic. Before leaving the construction site, workers demolished the pathology department, which was in the way of the new project, and from roughly mid-April the handling of the bodies of the deceased in Prešov changed significantly. Two hospital employees described "wartime conditions" at the pathology department to Denník N and provided photographs.

The demolished pathology department had six refrigeration boxes, each holding four bodies; the five-to-six-year-old boxes were taken to scrap by the workers before demolition. The deceased began to be placed in the so-called old pathology, which employees call the "cellar." The photographs show dead bodies in black bags placed on shelves among boards, boxes and plastic sheeting. After the photographs were sent, hospital management issued an internal instruction that returned the handling of bodies to its original state.

stories.title: Ministry presents university reform: rectors to be chosen by a new board

body.text: On Wednesday, universities were introduced to the ministry's proposal to transform how they operate. The election of the rector is to change — a new independent university board would select the rector, rather than the academic senates as is currently the case. Selection procedures for leadership posts would also be opened to candidates from abroad, and funding and the awarding of degrees would work differently.

The university board is to participate in strategies, the budget and the school's overall direction, but not to interfere in the content of teaching and research. It can appoint and dismiss the rector and, with two-thirds of its members, reject a candidate for dean. The board is to have nine members: three nominated by the academic senate, three external members selected by a commission appointed by the minister, and the remaining three chosen by the first six members. The chairman of the Czech Conference of Rectors, Milan Pospíšil, said the ministry had assured them that the external members should not be politicians currently in office.

Selection procedures for leadership posts would also be opened to candidates from abroad, and funding and the awarding of degrees would work differently.

stories.title: Upheaval at the Labour Ministry: deputy minister resigns, Trpkoš loses council seat

body.text: The powerful pair of officials behind the Ministry of Labour and Social Affairs has come under pressure. On Thursday, Kateřina Jirková stepped down from her position as the minister's political deputy, and the head of the IT section, Karel Trpkoš, had to leave the Government Council for Human Rights, to which Minister Aleš Juchelka (ANO) had nominated him just two weeks earlier. Juchelka confirmed his departure from the council.

Jirková cited personal reasons as well as long-term media pressure; she returns to her original position as director of the department for non-insurance social and family benefits. Trpkoš was brought to the ministry in 2022 by Marian Jurečka (KDU-ČSL) and was regarded as an indispensable expert on digitalisation. HN previously described how the tandem was the main driving force behind events and how its influence extended beyond the minister. The daily Alarm linked Trpkoš to a profile on the Lide.cz network, on which the author made racist remarks.

Juchelka confirmed his departure from the council.

stories.title: Trump promises Poland another 5,000 troops ahead of NATO ministers' meeting in Sweden

body.text: Even before US Secretary of State Marco Rubio spoke his first words at the top-level meeting with NATO partners in the Swedish city of Helsingborg, President Donald Trump had sent a conciliatory message: the US will send another 5,000 troops to Poland, which he justified by his good relationship with Polish President Karol Nawrocki. He provided no details, so it is unclear whether these are the troops he wants to withdraw from Germany or those whose rotation was recently halted.

Secretary General Mark Rutte welcomed the announcement but stressed that it did not change the direction that the alliance must become less dependent on a single member. Rubio confirmed that the president is rightly disappointed with some partners over the lack of support during the war with Iran. The meeting was the first summit with NATO partners since the American and Israeli attack on Iran. The US is expected to clarify the extent to which it will reduce forces for the secret NATO Force Model.

stories.title: Posselt defends CSU deputy chair Niebler against accusations of misusing EU funds

body.text: Former CSU MEP Bernd Posselt has defended the current MEP and deputy party chair Angelika Niebler against accusations that she may have misused EU funds for private purposes. Posselt, who himself features in the affair, called the accusations "unjustified and very artificial."

It is claimed that Niebler paid for an assistant for him from parliamentary funds, even though he was no longer a member of the European Parliament. Posselt denied this: his multilingual associate has worked for him full-time since 1989 and only had a small minijob with Niebler, looking after high-ranking guests in Strasbourg. Posselt also defended the decision of the EP's legal committee not to lift Niebler's immunity. According to him, the committee had the impression that the accusations came from an unsuccessful candidate who would like to get into parliament in Niebler's place. He described Niebler as one of the most diligent and competent members of parliament.

stories.title: Iran allegedly planned the murder of German politician Beck

body.text: The Federal Prosecutor's Office has charged the Dane Ali S. and the Afghan Tawab M. with preparing attacks in Germany, whose targets were to be the Green politician and president of the German-Israeli Society Volker Beck and the president of the Central Council of Jews in Germany, Josef Schuster. According to the indictment, the Dane worked for the secret service of the Iranian Revolutionary Guards. In early 2025 he allegedly began gathering information on Beck and Schuster, and by May at the latest established contact with the Afghan, who was willing to procure a weapon and induce an unknown third party to murder Beck.

Beck said he learned of the plan last summer and lived under strict protection for six weeks: without the police he could not even take out the rubbish, and an armoured vehicle accompanied him when walking his dog. He said he lived "like a tiger in a cage." Both men are in custody. Beck points out that the regime behind the plan still rules and that he therefore remains subject to special protective measures. Beck had received death threats before; six years ago the conspiracy theorist Attila Hildmann promised him the death penalty.

stories.title: Ankara court annuls election of Turkey's main opposition leader

body.text: An appeals court in Ankara on Thursday evening declared null and void all resolutions of the opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) congress from November 2023, including the election of chairman Özgür Özel and the rest of the leadership. To insure himself against the long-feared ruling, Özel had himself confirmed in office at an extraordinary congress in April 2025, with 1,171 of 1,276 delegates voting for him; he was re-elected by a similar majority in September 2025. The court has now declared these votes invalid as well.

A court of first instance had dismissed the lawsuits over alleged irregularities. According to the CHP, neither court is competent to assess the legality of the elections — that belongs only to the Supreme Electoral Council, which the party has approached and which convened an extraordinary session for Friday. The day after his ally Viktor Orbán's defeat in April, President Erdoğan declared that Turkish democracy would get the opposition party it deserves. Özel spoke on Thursday in front of CHP headquarters at a protest event.

stories.title: Washington indicts Raúl Castro as pressure on Havana mounts

body.text: The United States has indicted the 94-year-old former Cuban president and party chief Raúl Castro, holding him responsible for the 1996 shoot-down of two aircraft belonging to the exile organization Hermanos al Rescate. U.S. justice authorities accuse Castro and other defendants of, among other things, murder, conspiracy to kill U.S. citizens, and the destruction of aircraft. The Justice Department speaks of a long-overdue step.

The move recalls the action against Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro, whom the U.S. indicted for drug trafficking and "narcoterrorism." Before the military intervention of January 3, it significantly raised the bounty for his capture. Special forces then abducted Maduro and his wife Cilia Flores from the residence in Caracas, while the air force struck military facilities. Although there was no regime change — the country is now led by Maduro's deputy Delcy Rodríguez — Washington gained greater influence over the country with the world's largest oil reserves. Trump spoke of a "perfect scenario."

stories.title: Audit office: Half of subsidies for local road repairs were ineffective
stories.author: Martin Ťopek

body.text: Since 2019 until February 2025, the Ministry for Regional Development allocated municipalities a total of 4.7 billion crowns for repairs of local roads, even though municipalities themselves are supposed to cover the maintenance of these roads. A review by the Supreme Audit Office showed that in more than half of the inspected cases the support practically did not improve the traffic situation or the quality of life.

Auditor Jan Kinšt described the provision of such support as an ineffective expenditure of state funds and recommended discontinuing it. The ministry supported 2,150 projects; the auditors focused on twenty of them in 14 municipalities and in 12 cases found that the money went to insignificant roads — dead-end streets, roads in an allotment-garden colony, or peripheral parts of municipalities. An example is a dead-end street made of concrete panels in Opatovice nad Labem, whose traffic significance the municipality emphasized in its application. According to the office, clear rules were missing; some municipalities even used the subsidy to fund new driveways to family houses or traffic signage. Spokeswoman Hana Kadečková said the cases were selected at random.

shortBody.text: Since 2019 until February 2025, the Ministry for Regional Development allocated municipalities a total of 4.7 billion crowns for repairs of local roads, even though municipalities themselves are supposed to cover the maintenance of these roads. A review by the Supreme Audit Office showed that in more than half of the inspected cases the support practically did not improve the traffic situation or the quality of life.

stories.title: Havlíček: State is seeking a mechanism to fill gas storage for winter
stories.author: Jan Brož

body.text: Minister of Industry and Trade Karel Havlíček said in an interview that gas storage is currently filled to less than 20 percent, which is not extraordinary at the end of the heating season, but it needs to be refilled. The problem is that prices of contracts for summer gas delivery are higher than in winter, so filling storage does not make economic sense for traders.

Guarantees are therefore on the table, through which the state would vouch for filling the underground caverns. Havlíček said this is a problem for all of Europe and traders are waiting to see what mechanisms European states create. If the situation were unsustainable, the state can intervene in the regulated component of both electricity and gas. In the interview he also commented on personnel changes at ČEPS, from which Martin Durčák was recalled and Jan Kalina was elected to the board, whom the minister expects to be elected chairman. According to Havlíček, Durčák had served at ČEPS for eight to nine years, and after such a long period the time had come for a change.

stories.title: Plaga's secondary school reform stalls, merging of fields failed

body.text: One of the big plans of the former leadership of the Ministry of Education – to transform the range of secondary school fields and cut or merge most of the current 283 – has not been fulfilled. For example, the fields of carpenter and roofer were successfully merged, while in food processing the fields of food technology and food analysis remained separate.

According to critics, school principals had too much influence and little of the original visions was achieved; Marcela Davídková Antošová, representative of the electrical engineering fields, eventually left the working group. Among the original intentions was an apprenticeship certificate with the value of a school-leaving exam (maturita) and a professional maturita. The reform is now at a standstill – the National Pedagogical Institute is waiting for instructions from the ministry. According to the former leadership, the popularity of lycea at least rose, and a new field of general lyceum was introduced, which is being tested at dozens of schools.

stories.title: Teslamp bankruptcy after 24 years: receiver to pay a hundred million instead of billions

body.text: The bankruptcy proceedings of one of the largest lighting manufacturers in Central Europe, the company Teslamp Holešovice, are not over even 24 years after its collapse. They are obstructed by a lawsuit over who is to blame for the collapse. Bankruptcy receiver Pavel Klán blames Česká spořitelna, which allegedly pulled out of the company money lent to its privatizers, and sued it for 3.7 billion crowns.

However, the District Court for Prague 7 dismissed the lawsuit last Thursday because it failed to prove either the occurrence or the amount of the damage. It rejected the motion by Teslamp's legal representative Tomáš Ferenc for further evidence and a new expert report, and described his approach as purposeful and obstructive, and his conduct as unethical. The case has its roots in the management privatization of the mid-1990s, when the company TES Praha bought the enterprise for 893 million crowns. The former factory near the Libeň Bridge is now a parking lot.

stories.title: NSA chief wants sports subsidies tied also to membership numbers and resilience

body.text: The National Sports Agency plans to overhaul its subsidy programmes. According to its chief, who has been in charge of sports funding since the days of Karel Kovář, it should not be medals alone that decide, but also the number of members. The agency is working on a new programme period and has discussed the financial framework for the years 2028 to 2035.

Last July the government approved a strategy in which sport did not appear, but it does contain the topic of resilience, which the NSA wants to add to the document. It is negotiating with the ministries of education, defence and regional development so that European funds can ease the budget.

The agency wants to allocate support to sports with the potential for a broad membership base – swimming, athletics, cycling – as well as to small disciplines that bring success, such as speed skating or snowboarding. It divides large sports facilities into those for representation and those for general use, or sports venues following the Polish model.

stories.title: Ukraine strikes with drones deep inside Russia, experts expect a Russian spring offensive

body.text: Footage of a burning oil terminal in Primorsk in the Leningrad region, which circulated on social media on Monday, showed a new intensity of Ukrainian strikes. Ukraine sends hundreds of drones every day against targets deep inside Russia.

According to Carnegie Europe analyst Bielieskov, the main new trend is striking Russian targets up to roughly a hundred kilometres from the front, where there is large-scale destruction of expensive equipment in the Russian rear. Missiles have also hit arms factories more than a thousand kilometres deep inside Russian territory, and for several days in a row Moscow's air defences were kept busy.

President Volodymyr Zelensky set the goal of making the Russians feel the war as much as possible. At the same time, experts warn that the Russians are waiting with larger ground operations until nature turns green and provides them with cover – the campaign usually begins in the second half of April and lasts until October.

stories.title: Warsh wants the Fed to talk less. He promises a "regime change"

body.text: The US Senate has confirmed Kevin Warsh as the next Federal Reserve chair. Warsh is assuming the position armed with a pledge to unleash "regime change" that is supposed to bolster the central bank's credibility. He faces an exceptionally difficult task: President Donald Trump is demanding big interest rate cuts, which, according to a recent Group of Thirty report, risks undermining the Fed's independence.

Meanwhile, consumer price inflation has just hit 3.8 per cent, producer price inflation 6 per cent, and ten-year bond yields are approaching 4.5 per cent. Warsh proposes shrinking the Fed's balance sheet, coordinating better with the Treasury and narrowing the central bank's mission. He also hopes the artificial intelligence boom will bring productivity growth that allows rates to be cut. A less-watched element of his plan is communication reform. According to Warsh, once policymakers publish their economic forecast, they become prisoners of their own words and cling to predictions for too long. In his view, the Fed tells the whole world what its "dots" will look like.

stories.title: Britain in a debt cage: gilts decide the government's fate

body.text: Hours before Britain's government was rocked by resignation, Chancellor Rachel Reeves stepped in front of the Downing Street cameras to warn that a Labour leadership battle risked "plunging the country into chaos". Wes Streeting quit as health secretary during the day. Investors saw her comments as the government's latest attempt to use the threat of a bond sell-off to stave off internal rebellion. According to one Labour official, this would be the first party leadership contest where the bond market has a vote.

The market for gilts punishes any deviation from fiscal discipline. Mike Riddell of Fidelity International warns of a "debt loop" in which markets raise borrowing costs, which slows growth and increases the need for further borrowing. Britain has the highest borrowing costs in the G7. This week, as leadership disputes deepened and investors feared a shift of Labour to the left, the ten-year bond yield reached a post-crisis high above 5.1 per cent.

stories.title: Trump flattered, Xi drew the lines. Taiwan as the red line

body.text: On the first day of his visit to Beijing, President Donald Trump focused on his personal relationship with Xi Jinping. "You're a great leader," he told his host, whom he has often said he admires for his "powerful" control over a nation of 1.4 billion people. Xi, by contrast, spent little time on flattery. Once the 21-gun salute and precision-marching by units of the People's Liberation Army were finished, he plunged right away into setting boundaries. The red line, he said, was Taiwan.

"The U.S. must handle the Taiwan issue with utmost caution," he said according to a readout from Xinhua, China's official news agency, just minutes into his public remarks in the Great Hall of the People. Trump appeared conciliatory, in stark contrast to his rhetoric about China at home, where during his campaigns he had branded it a thief of jobs and a security threat. Xi, meanwhile, smiled but was more confrontational, signaling that despite deflation, depopulation and a burst real estate bubble, China presents itself as an equal superpower.

stories.title: Slavia in turmoil after abandoned derby, Tvrdík offered resignation

body.text: Slavia Prague's footballers defended their championship title after a 5:1 win over Jablonec, but the club is going through its most difficult period in a long time. On Saturday evening, a frenzied crowd with flares stormed onto the pitch at the Eden stadium and the match was left unfinished.

A few hours after the match, the executive official Jaroslav Tvrdík came to owner Pavel Tykač with an offer to resign from his position as the club's head. Tykač, ruler of the energy group Sev.en, who bought Slavia three years ago, rejected the offer without hesitation, saying that everyone makes a mistake from time to time and that he has no replacement for the man running the club in his specific micromanagement style. The abandoned derby is a blow to all of Czech club football, which has grown as a product in recent years and attracted new owners and capital in the form of increased television and marketing revenues. Slavia has won six titles in the last ten years.

stories.title: How to reduce capital gains tax on long-term saving in Germany

body.text: Many Germans save for retirement the most convenient way: through broadly diversified, low-cost ETFs. Thanks to the compound interest effect, this is a lucrative strategy, but it is not always the most tax-efficient. Capital gains – interest, dividends and price gains – are subject to a 25 per cent withholding tax plus a 5.5 per cent solidarity surcharge and possibly church tax, together up to 26.375 per cent.

Tax adviser Christian Herold of the Steuerrat24.de portal advises using the saver's allowance, which is 1,000 euros per year for individuals and 2,000 euros for jointly assessed couples; up to this amount the gains are tax-free. Jana Bauerová of the Bundesverband Lohnsteuerhilfevereine recommends fully using up the allowance every year – someone who annually sells part of their holdings and immediately reinvests realizes up to 1,000 euros of gains tax-free. Someone who does nothing for ten years pays around 2,600 euros on the same amount.

stories.title: Nedvěd: Bank results on the Prague exchange again did not disappoint
stories.author: Karel Nedvěd

body.text: According to Karel Nedvěd of Fio banka, among the certainties that investors can rely on in uncertain times are the solid results of the three banks traded on the Prague Stock Exchange and in the RM-System. This was confirmed when they reported their figures for the fourth quarter, or the full year 2025.

The largest of them, Erste Group, posted its highest-ever net profit for last year. According to Nedvěd, however, the key information was not just the current quarterly results, but above all the successful completion of the acquisition of Poland's Santander. Erste shares on the Prague exchange closed at 2,278 korunas with a daily gain of 3.45 percent and make up 19.09 percent of the PX index. Komerční banka ended at 1,105 korunas, Moneta at 191.40 korunas with a rise of 2.79 percent. The PX index closed at 2,536.93 points, a slight decline of 0.12 percent.

stories.title: Hrstková: Does the Czech Republic face another inflation jump?
stories.author: Julie Hrstková

body.text: According to Julie Hrstková, rising oil prices are gradually feeding into the price lists at filling stations and raising the question of another inflation jump. The International Energy Agency issued recommendations on how to mitigate the economic impact of expensive oil — working from home, lowering speed limits on motorways by at least 10 kilometers per hour, supporting public transport, sharing cars, and limiting private trips.

These recommendations, however, have so far not met with understanding, according to her. A number of European politicians stated that they see no reason yet to call on citizens to limit their use of cars. The Czech government of Andrej Babiš is also not planning any measures; the Ministry of Industry and Trade together with the Ministry of Finance is still analyzing the situation. The investment bank Goldman Sachs has already, for the second time in less than two weeks, raised its oil price estimate due to disruptions to supplies in the Strait of Hormuz and rising structural risks.

stories.title: Šuhajdová: The Czech economy grew three times faster than Slovakia's last year
stories.author: Anna Šuhajdová

body.text: According to Anna Šuhajdová, last year's economic development in the Czech Republic and Slovakia was very different, and the same should hold true this year. While the Czech economy was among the fastest-growing developed countries not just within the EU, the Slovak economy effectively stagnated.

The gross domestic product of the Czech Republic rose by 2.6 percent year on year last year. According to Czech statisticians, this growth was driven above all by investment, which added 5.3 percent year on year, and household consumption also helped, rising by more than three percent. The Czech economy should maintain its solid condition going forward, according to Šuhajdová, while the Slovak economy is expected to lag behind. A factor in the background is also rising oil prices linked to tensions in the Strait of Hormuz.

stories.title: Kadera: No offices are being built in Prague, two trends drive the slowdown
stories.author: Pavel Kadera

body.text: According to Pavel Kadera, the Prague office market is experiencing a significant slowdown in new construction. For this year, according to an analysis by Knight Frank, only 36,700 square meters of new office space is planned for completion, which corresponds to roughly two medium-sized buildings.

Brownfields in sought-after locations are rapidly disappearing, and developers are increasingly converting those that remain into residential projects. The office vacancy rate fell to 5.9 percent, the lowest level since the start of 2020. In Prague 2 it is just 1.8 percent, and in Prague 8 it is 3.3 percent. In more distant locations such as Prague 4 or 5, where rents hover around 15–16 euros per square meter, construction does not pay off; for projects to make sense, rents would have to rise by roughly 25 percent. The construction of purely office complexes will therefore stagnate, with the exception of the most lucrative addresses.

stories.title: Investment bubbles are a common feature of financial markets
stories.author: Michal Stupavský

body.text: According to Michal Stupavský, financial markets regularly go through periods of euphoria and disillusionment, when asset prices rise to seemingly unlimited heights, only to painfully return to reality afterward. The term "investment bubble" is therefore among the most discussed topics among professional investors as well as small savers.

Although individual approaches differ, most investing legends agree on one point: long-term returns are determined by the entry valuation, that is, the valuation at the moment an asset is purchased. Stupavský recalls that investors' perception of reality itself influences the fundamentals — rising stock prices improve companies' access to capital, which in turn supports profit growth and justifies higher valuations. This self-referential process, however, is not endless; once expectations break away from reality too far, a turning point occurs. He also refers to Soros's theory of reflexivity.

stories.title: Concerns over corporate debt and AI's impact on the credit system
stories.author: Jiří Król

body.text: According to Jiří Król, debate about the private credit market has intensified in recent weeks. Rising redemptions from some funds, questions about the valuation of portfolios, and concerns about the impact of artificial intelligence on the software sector have led to speculation about whether the credit system that grew up alongside banks after the financial crisis is beginning to crack.

Król, however, considers such a dramatic interpretation misleading. In his view, the market is going through a period of higher volatility and investor nervousness, which is natural in an environment of technological change, still relatively high interest rates, and geopolitical tension. He argues that elevated volatility alone does not yet signal a systemic problem in the quality of corporate debt. He asks whether concerns about corporate debt are really justified, and how things stand with its quality and with the real impact of artificial intelligence on the sector.
